name: Divine guardians
shortDescription: Divine guardians in Buddhism protect the Dharma, sacred sites, and devotees. They are often depicted as wrathful deities warding off evil and ignorance.
description: Divine guardians in Buddhist traditions encompass a diverse range of deities, spirits, and enlightened beings tasked with protecting the Dharma (Buddhist teachings), sacred spaces, and practitioners. These guardians are not simply passive protectors; they actively combat negative influences, both internal (such as ignorance and delusion) and external (such as malevolent spirits and destructive forces). Their origins stem from pre-Buddhist local deities and spirits assimilated into the Buddhist pantheon, as well as enlightened beings like Bodhisattvas who vow to safeguard the Dharma. Many are depicted as wrathful deities (Herukas) with fierce countenances, symbolic weapons, and dynamic postures, representing the energetic force needed to overcome obstacles on the path to enlightenment. These guardians are often associated with specific directions, temples, or practices, and are invoked for protection, blessings, and the removal of obstacles. Some prominent examples include the Four Heavenly Kings (Lokapalas), each guarding a cardinal direction, and Mahakala, a wrathful manifestation of Avalokiteshvara (Chenrezig), the Bodhisattva of compassion. The role of divine guardians is crucial in maintaining the integrity of Buddhist teachings and providing a safe environment for spiritual practice, ensuring the continuity of the Dharma for future generations.

sources.title: The Princeton Dictionary of Buddhism
sources.author: Robert E. Buswell Jr. and Donald S. Lopez Jr.
